pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

PERSONAL TRAINING CLIENT INTAKE FORM
Please fill in and save this form in Word, Google Docs or ODS.
The more information you provide, the better I will be able to assist you.
You can use either the imperial or metric system to fill in this form. Just
state which unit you used (e.g. cm, kg, lb, inch).

General
 Full legal name: M
 Age: 32
 Height: 178cm
 Weight: 72
 Body fat percentage (plus estimation method): around 14% (3 point
caliper)
 Years of training experience: about 8

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

Strength
For the section below, please give your current maximum abilities in
weight times reps, e.g. 250 lb x 6.
 Bench press: 1RM = 95kg
 Squat (excluding body weight): 1RM = 110kg
 Chin-up (excluding body weight): I don't have a weight belt
 Overhead press: 1RM = 45kg

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

What are your fitness goals?
Loose the belly fat, build muscle to reach my full potential.

How often in a week would you be prepared to train for maximal
results?
2

What is your occupation?
You may be general here, e.g. student/manager/personal trainer/manual
labor/doctor. The aim is to characterize your circadian rhythm, stress and
activity level.
Self-employed office job. Working on the computer.

Please list any pathologies and injuries (disabilities, allergies,
illnesses, syndromes, disorders etc.) you have or had:
Lumbar disc herniation (4+5) about two years ago. Since then, lifting
heavy is a problem on squats and deadlifts (which I don't do anymore
anyway)

Do you insist on any special diet (e.g. ketogenic, vegan)?
Vegetarian preferred

Do you plan to use any PEDs while working together? If so, please
list them:
No. I think natural limits are there for a reason.

Lifestyle
Are there any times at which you are unable or unwilling to train?
(This includes when you're at work!) If so, please list them:
no

Activity level
Please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
 Sedentary (e.g. office job)
 Somewhat active (e.g. you walk your dog several times a day or you
commute by bicycle)
 Active (e.g. full-time PT, literally on your feet most of the day)
 Very active (e.g. involved in manual labor)

Stress level
Please mark one of the fields below or delete the rest.
At this point, you may choose to return this form to me to obtain a
price quote before filling in the rest. Please attach at least two full-body
pictures of yourself in clear lighting, preferably including a back double biceps
and front relaxed pose.

Legal disclaimer: By returning this intake form, you declare that while Menno
Henselmans is your personal trainer, all the substances you use are obtained
and used in accordance with their governing laws and you forego all rights to
hold Menno Henselmans liable for any damages caused in relation to following
his advice as your trainer.

Bedtime: Between 10 & 11 pm
Wakeup time: around 7.30 am

How much caffeine do you consume daily on average or on a

2-3 Cups of coffee

Equipment

Yes, a plastic one.

1.25kg

What are the smallest weight increments between the sets of
dumbbells you have access to?
1kg steps for 1kg to 10kg, after that the increments are 2kg.

 a reverse hyper machine? No

 a standing calf raise machine? Sort of
 a dip/chin-up belt? yes

Yes, should be the exact same as on the image
Does your available equipment differ from that in most gyms in
any other way (e.g. you train in a Crossfit box or at home)?
No. But I've got access to a barbell with some weights, dumbbells and a
medicine ball (12kg). I've got this stuff at home for an afternoon session.

Supplements

Whey sometimes

Genetics
 Wrist circumference (smallest point): 17cm
 Ankle circumference (smallest point): 23cm
 How much did you weigh before you started lifting and what was
your height at that point? I don't know anymore. But I think I was
the same height as now, 178cm
 Were you heavy as a baby? No, I was more on the lower end
 Please attach a picture to this form in which the back of your right
hand is clearly visible. Ideally, place your right hand on a flat
surface with your fingers touching each other and then take a
picture of it.

Current Program
Please describe or attach your current diet plan in detail:
I've tried a bulk over the last 10 weeks. I think I've gained a lot of fat…
Starting point was 73kg at about 8% bodyfat (3 point caliper measure

My Plan looked as follows:
- Week 1: 2500kcal/day
- Week 2-5: 2650kcal/day -> 77,1kg bodyfat around 10%

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

- Week 6&7: 2550kcal/day -> Weight stayed pretty much the same
- Week 8: 2600kcal/day -> 77,5kg
- Week 9: 2650kcal/day -> 77,3kg bodyfat around 13,5%
My Macros were like this: 50% Carbs, 25% Fats, 25% Protein
My typical food choices over the day:
- Breakfast:
o Wholegrain bread or toast with cheese and eggs on top
o smoothie consisting of wholegrain oats, apple, banana,
cinnamon and a scoop whey
- Lunch:
o Meat like soy or milk thingy with veggies and rice, noodles or
potatoes
o Lentils
o Beans
- Dinner:
o Mozzarella with tomatoes
o toast with cheese
o red peppers
o salad
- Snacks:
o Nuts
o Almonds
o Protein Bar if I'm out of office

- Week 10: started cutting with keto diet on 3000kcal/day for
adaptation phase -> Failed (First week was ok, 2nd week I was a
mess, legs were cramping walking stairs, I couldn't concentrate
anymore, therefore couldn't work. So I stopped it.
My Split was like this:

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

C: 5%
F: 75%
P: 20%

During the "bulk" I did Crossfit like excercises combined with compound
lifts 3 days/week.

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

A day looked like this:

Warm-Up Row 2k
Lift heavy on a compound movement (BenchPress, Squat, Deadlift)
o Six sets with increasing weights and decreasing reps of Squats
o Six sets with increasing weights and decreasing reps of
Deadlifts
o Six sets with increasing weights and decreasing reps of Bench
Press
o 100 Thrusters or Power Cleans
o 50 Pullups or Dips or Burpees
I mixed this workout week by week with this:
Day 1:

Day 2:

Day 3:
Deadlifts

5x3 100kg
5x3 75kg
5x3 75kg
Bench Press
4x4 85kg
5x3 40kg
4x4 105kg
Squat

3x10 85kg
4x4 95kg
3x10 40kg
Overhead Press 4x4 45kg
3x10 90kg
3x10 75kg
Pullups

4xMax

4xMax

4xMax
I've played with the rest between sets and tried to reduce it whenever
possible. This program though fatigued me extremely. And my back
started to tighten up.
1,5 weeks rest.

My current Program I'm doing for about 1 week now looks like this:
As I'm still suffering from the Keto-fail I struggle through…
Now I'm at 2300kcal/day with a split of C40, P20, F40.

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

The 1 RM I planned for every 3 weeks.
I've eliminated the Deadlifts! Squats only once a week, to help my back
recover.

Please return the filled in form to Coaching@BayesianBodybuilding.com
and attach at least two full-body pictures of yourself in clear lighting
(without excessive shadows; not your most flattering Facebook selfies),
preferably including a back double biceps and front relaxed pose. If you
recently had blood work done, please attach its results as well.

BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

Client assessment
Plugging his data into the Bayesian PT Course calculator gives the following result.

With likely impulsive eating habits, a low training frequency and a high body fat percentage, I
recommend 4 meals a day with an early breakfast.

Since he's still far removed from his genetic limitations and he only has 2 workouts per
week, we can expect high MPS in the anabolic window for at least 24 hours. Outside of that,
we can go with a moderate PSMF with kcal = 9.7 * protein intake, setting protein intake at
73 kg * 0.4 g/kg = 29 g. With a total protein intake of 72 kg * 1.8 g/kg/d = 130 g protein, that
gives us his 3 weekly PSMF rest days.

Our target weekly energy intake is 7 * 1727 = 12089 kcal. With the rest days subtracted, we
have 12089 - 3 * 1261 = 8306 kcal left. If he trains after meal 2, his anabolic window spans
right across 2 days. That's 8306 / 4 = 2077 kcal per day.

With 8 years of training experience and still close to novice level strength, a body fat
percentage close to overweight status and a willingness to do no more than 2 workouts per
week, it's safe to say diet adherence will be problematic. So to make meal planning and
adherence easy, I simplified his diet to have only 2 different meal macros: bulk and cut meals
with bulk meals from the pre-workout meal until meal 3 the next day about 24 hours after
Rest day after rest day (3x)

With likely impulsive eating habits, a low training frequency and a high body fat percentage, I
recommend 4 meals a day with an early breakfast.
Since he’s still far removed from his genetic limitations and he only has 2 workouts per
week, we can expect high MPS in the anabolic window for at least 24 hours. Outside of that,
we can go with a moderate PSMF with kcal = 9.7 * protein intake, setting protein intake at
73 kg * 0.4 g/kg = 29 g. With a total protein intake of 72 kg * 1.8 g/kg/d = 130 g protein, that
gives us his 3 weekly PSMF rest days.
Our target weekly energy intake is 7 * 1727 = 12089 kcal. With the rest days subtracted, we
have 12089 - 3 * 1261 = 8306 kcal left. If he trains after meal 2, his anabolic window spans
right across 2 days. That’s 8306 / 4 = 2077 kcal per day.
With 8 years of training experience and still close to novice level strength, a body fat
percentage close to overweight status and a willingness to do no more than 2 workouts per
week, it’s safe to say diet adherence will be problematic. So to make meal planning and
adherence easy, I simplified his diet to have only 2 different meal macros: bulk and cut meals
with bulk meals from the pre-workout meal until meal 3 the next day about 24 hours after

pages.text:
BAYESIANBODYBUILDING.COM

that meal. That's 3 bulk meals 2x per week with a 281 kcal PSMF meal each day, giving (2077
- 281) / 3 = 599 kcal per bulk meal.

Since he's vegetarian, to make up for reduced protein quality, I added 20% to the usual 1.8
g/kg/d protein requirement on the 4 days in his anabolic window, giving 1.8 g/kg/d * 1.2 * 72
kg = 156 g protein on those days. Without the PSMF meal, that's (155 - 29) / 3 = 42 g
protein per bulk meal.

Fat intake I set at 40% of bulk day energy intake: 0.4 * 2078 / 9 = 92 g fat. That's a bit tough
for compliance for him perhaps, but he needs a lot of fat as a vegetarian and his rest days will
probably have almost no fat.

To keep things relatively simple, I gave no instructions for the distribution of fat or
carbohydrate intake per meal at first, giving the following macro set-up with the following
instructions.

Your program has bulk and cut meals.
The first 6 meals after each training session and the first meal before it are bulk meals.
Any meals after that until your next training session are cut meals.
Cut meals/days are protein sparing modified fasts (PSMFs).
This means you should aim to minimize caloric intake.
This effectively means your calories are maxima and your protein intake is a minimum.
Don't worry about the carb:fat ratio.
During bulk meals, your calorie intake is a fixed target. During bulk periods you also want to ensure a sufficient daily fat intake.
Additionally, it's helpful to make your last meal of the day relatively high carb.
Blanks in the macro overview indicate there is no target: as long as all other targets are met, this could be any number.

Client assessment
Plugging his data into the Bayesian PT Course calculator gives the following result.

With likely impulsive eating habits, a low training frequency and a high body fat percentage, I
recommend 4 meals a day with an early breakfast.

Since he's still far removed from his genetic limitations and he only has 2 workouts per
week, we can expect high MPS in the anabolic window for at least 24 hours. Outside of that,
we can go with a moderate PSMF with kcal = 9.7 * protein intake, setting protein intake at
73 kg * 0.4 g/kg = 29 g. With a total protein intake of 72 kg * 1.8 g/kg/d = 130 g protein, that
gives us his 3 weekly PSMF rest days.

Our target weekly energy intake is 7 * 1727 = 12089 kcal. With the rest days subtracted, we
have 12089 - 3 * 1261 = 8306 kcal left. If he trains after meal 2, his anabolic window spans
right across 2 days. That's 8306 / 4 = 2077 kcal per day.

With 8 years of training experience and still close to novice level strength, a body fat
percentage close to overweight status and a willingness to do no more than 2 workouts per
week, it's safe to say diet adherence will be problematic. So to make meal planning and
adherence easy, I simplified his diet to have only 2 different meal macros: bulk and cut meals
with bulk meals from the pre-workout meal until meal 3 the next day about 24 hours after

- 281) / 3 = 599 kcal per bulk meal.

Since he's vegetarian, to make up for reduced protein quality, I added 20% to the usual 1.8
g/kg/d protein requirement on the 4 days in his anabolic window, giving 1.8 g/kg/d * 1.2 * 72
kg = 156 g protein on those days. Without the PSMF meal, that's (155 - 29) / 3 = 42 g
protein per bulk meal.

Fat intake I set at 40% of bulk day energy intake: 0.4 * 2078 / 9 = 92 g fat. That's a bit tough
for compliance for him perhaps, but he needs a lot of fat as a vegetarian and his rest days will
probably have almost no fat.

To keep things relatively simple, I gave no instructions for the distribution of fat or
carbohydrate intake per meal at first, giving the following macro set-up with the following
instructions.

Your program has bulk and cut meals.
The first 6 meals after each training session and the first meal before it are bulk meals.
Any meals after that until your next training session are cut meals.
Cut meals/days are protein sparing modified fasts (PSMFs).
This means you should aim to minimize caloric intake.
This effectively means your calories are maxima and your protein intake is a minimum.
Don't worry about the carb:fat ratio.
During bulk meals, your calorie intake is a fixed target. During bulk periods you also want to ensure a sufficient daily fat intake.
Additionally, it's helpful to make your last meal of the day relatively high carb.
Blanks in the macro overview indicate there is no target: as long as all other targets are met, this could be any number.
